1: shadow Hear my voice, O God, in my complaint; preserve my life from dread of the enemy. God hears Christ's voice in the complaint of Gethsemane — with loud cries and tears, to him who was able to save him from death, and he was heard (Hebrews 5:7). The preservation of his life comes not by averting death but by raising him through it.
2: shadow Hide me from the secret plots of the wicked, from the throng of evildoers, The Sanhedrin's secret conspiracy: the chief priests and the scribes were seeking how to put him to death, for they feared the people (Luke 22:2). Their secret plot against Christ is the supreme instance of this verse — the righteous one surrounded by hidden conspirators.
3: shadow who whet their tongues like swords, who aim bitter words like arrows, The verbal weapons at the trial: false witnesses (Matthew 26:59-61), the high priest's accusations (Matthew 26:65), the soldiers' taunts (Luke 23:36), the crowd's mockery (Matthew 27:39-43). Tongues sharpened like swords are aimed at the one who is the Word of God — the living Word against whom words are weaponized.
4: shadow shooting from ambush at the blameless, shooting at him suddenly and without fear. The arrest in Gethsemane is the ambush — Judas and the crowd arriving suddenly in the night (John 18:3). The blameless one is struck without warning by those who have no fear of God before their eyes (Romans 3:18, citing Psalms 36:1). The ambush of the Son of God is the ultimate crime of the age.
5: shadow They hold fast to their evil purpose; they talk of laying snares secretly, thinking, Who can see us? Who can see us? — they do not reckon with the one who sees in secret (Matthew 6:4) and before whom all things are naked and exposed (Hebrews 4:13). The Sanhedrin thought their night trial and secret burial sealed would go unwitnessed by God — but the resurrection reveals all.
6: shadow They search out injustice, saying, We have accomplished a diligent search. For the inward thought and heart of a man is deep. The deep inward scheming of those who plotted against Christ — their careful search for a legal pretext (Luke 22:2), the false witnesses sought (Matthew 26:59). They thought they had accomplished their purpose; the resurrection overturns every deep scheme they devised.
7: shadow But God shoots his arrow at them; they are wounded suddenly. The divine reversal at the resurrection: those who shot arrows at the blameless are suddenly wounded by God's own action. The stone rolled away, the guards trembling like dead men (Matthew 28:4), the reports of resurrection spreading despite sealed tombs — God shoots his arrow, and the plotters are undone.
8: shadow They are brought to ruin, with their own tongues turned against them; all who see them will wag their heads. The self-defeating plots of the Passion: Judas hangs himself (Matthew 27:5); Pilate's inscription King of the Jews remains unchanged despite priestly objection (John 19:21-22); the sealed tomb is broken open. Their own measures turn against them and vindicate the one they condemned.
9: shadow Then all mankind fears; they tell what God has brought about and ponder what he has done. The centurion's response at the cross: Truly this was the Son of God! (Matthew 27:54). The fear that falls on all — the guards, the women, the disciples at the empty tomb — is the fear of beholding what God has brought about. The pondering of God's deeds is the first movement of Christian proclamation.
10: shadow Let the righteous one rejoice in the LORD and take refuge in him! Let all the upright in heart exult! The righteous one who rejoices is Christ — raised from the dead, the firstfruits of those who have fallen asleep (1 Corinthians 15:20). All the upright in heart exult because his resurrection is their own: if the Spirit of him who raised Jesus from the dead dwells in you (Romans 8:11), the same exultation is theirs.
